ATP Tour · 2000–2026 · R / XGBoost / dplyr

Can a model built from public match data beat the bookmaker's own price? The honest answer is no — and proving that took more rigor than finding a fake edge would have.

A full pipeline from raw, messy ATP data to a calibrated XGBoost win‑probability model, stress‑tested four independent ways against the market. This is a write‑up of the process: the data forensics, the leakage‑safe feature engineering, and the falsification tests that separate a real signal from a lucky backtest.

Matches modeled
≈65,900
Forward‑test window
2025–26 N=3,378
Independent falsification tests
4
Verdict
No edge vs. closing odds
01 · Motivation 02 · Data forensics 03 · Feature engineering 04 · Modeling & calibration 05 · Model vs. market 06 · Four falsification tests 07 · Supplementary: rank alone 08 · Conclusion 09 · Next steps
01 / Motivation

Betting markets are a good sandbox for a question every data scientist eventually has to face.

"My backtest looks profitable — is that a real edge, or did I just get lucky on this sample?"

Sports betting markets are a useful, low‑stakes place to practice answering that question honestly, because there's a built‑in, hard‑to‑argue‑with baseline — the bookmaker's own implied probability — and a clear, falsifiable claim to test against it: does my model beat that baseline, out of sample, after the market has already closed?

This project documents the full journey: cleaning genuinely messy raw data, engineering leakage‑safe features, training and calibrating a gradient‑boosted model, and then — the part most portfolio projects skip — a deliberate attempt to disprove my own promising‑looking results before trusting them.

Time‑series‑safe validation Surface‑specific Elo Platt scaling Proper scoring rules (log loss / Brier / AUC / ECE) Horse‑race regression Bootstrap confidence intervals R · dplyr · xgboost · tidyr · pROC
02 / Data forensics

Before touching a single model, the raw data got audited like evidence — not just scanned for blanks.

The source is ATP men's tour match‑level data (2000–2026): results, scores, rankings, ranking points, tournament metadata, and bookmaker odds. Several real issues surfaced during cleaning, each handled deliberately rather than dropped by default:

  • ~3,800 rows carried sentinel/placeholder odds values. These needed careful triage — a genuinely missing price is not the same thing as a legitimate extreme‑favorite price of 1.01, and collapsing the two would quietly bias the favorite/underdog balance of the whole dataset.
  • 6 rows had odds entered on the wrong scale — fractional (<1) instead of decimal — and were inverted back onto the correct scale rather than dropped.
  • ~1,785 raw player name strings collapsed to ~1,652 real players after normalization: initials, hyphenation, diacritics, and a hand‑built dictionary of known typos and formatting variants (built from manually reviewing near‑duplicate names).
  • 1,632 Carpet‑surface matches dropped — the surface was discontinued on tour after 2008 and is no longer representative of current playing conditions.

Anomaly caught by a structural sanity check, not a null‑value scan

Average ranking points for the 501–1000 rank tier in 2023 came out higher than the 251–500 tier — a mathematical impossibility given how ATP ranking points are structured (points strictly decrease as rank number increases). Comparing an aggregate against what's structurally possible, not just checking for NAs, is what caught this.

330.8 in 2023 vs. 49.3 the following year, and ~30–56 in every other year on record — an isolated step‑change in a single tier, a single year. Filtering down to that year/tier combination traced the error to two tournaments (Lyon Open, Geneva Open) with corrupted rank/points fields, corrected manually via a row‑level lookup table rather than a blanket rule.

Two more corrections worth naming because of what was deliberately kept, not removed: rows where Pts = 1 (genuine tour rookies), Rank = 1 (world No. 1), and Odd ≈ 1 (extreme betting favorites) were all kept as real signal — the temptation to treat every boundary value as "probably an error" is itself a data‑cleaning trap.

03 / Feature engineering

Every feature is computed strictly forward in time — nothing a model sees is allowed to know its own outcome.

The dataset is a time series of dependent observations (the same players reappear constantly), so the single hardest constraint on this project was no lookahead: every rating, rate, and count a match uses must be built only from information available before that match was played.

Elo engine
3 surface‑specific ladders

Hard / Clay / Grass Elo tracked independently and updated match‑by‑match in strict chronological order, so a player's clay‑court form never leaks into their hard‑court rating.

Adaptive K‑factor
40 → 20 after 10 matches

New or returning players' ratings move faster for their first 10 matches on a surface, then settle into a slower, more stable update rate.

  • Log‑rank differential between opponents (log rank compresses the huge gap between, say, rank 3 and rank 30 relative to rank 300 vs. 327).
  • Career maturity differential — total matches played, as a proxy for experience gap.
  • First‑set "momentum" features — each player's trailing (lagged) rate of winning the first set, and their trailing recovery rate after losing it, combined into a directional Momentum_Edge_P1 feature. "Trailing" and "lagged" are the operative words: a player's rate is computed from matches strictly before the current one.
  • Tournament context — ordinal weights for tournament tier (Slam → Masters → ATP500 → ATP250) and round depth (1st Round → Final).
  • Head‑to‑head features (raw and surface‑specific) were engineered and tested but consistently failed to improve out‑of‑sample performance, so they were dropped from the final feature set — a negative result kept visible rather than silently discarded.

Sanity check: does every feature actually move the outcome the way it should?

Before trusting any feature to a model, each was bucketed into deciles and plotted against the real win rate. A feature with a real, monotonic relationship to the outcome should trace a smooth, one‑directional line — anything flat or non‑monotonic is either a construction bug or a feature not worth keeping.

Elo, log‑rank, and momentum all trace clean, monotonic lines — kept. Two experimental workload features (rest days and recent set‑load differentials, not shown in the final feature list) were flat across the middle deciles and only moved at the extremes — a sign of a weak, threshold‑only relationship rather than a genuinely linear one — and were left out of the production feature set.
04 / Modeling & calibration

Chronological split, gradient boosting on public features only, then a calibration step the test period never sees.

The market's own odds are deliberately excluded from training and used solely as an evaluation benchmark — the entire point of the project is testing whether public match‑context features can independently compete with that number.

2009 – 2022
Train. XGBoost fit on public features only. Market odds withheld.
2023 – 2024
Calibrate. Platt scaling (logistic recalibration) fit on held‑out logits.
2025 – 2026
Forward‑test. Touched exactly once, at the very end.
# binary:logistic, shallow trees, moderate regularization — # deliberately conservative to avoid overfitting a noisy binary target xgb_params <- list( objective = "binary:logistic", eval_metric = "logloss", eta = 0.03, max_depth = 4, subsample = 0.8, colsample_bytree = 0.8, min_child_weight = 3 ) # Platt scaling: rescale the model's raw, often overconfident # probabilities using a logistic fit on the calibration period — # before the forward-test period is ever touched. calibration_fit <- glm(P1_Won ~ Logit_P1, data = calib_pool, family = binomial(link = "logit"))

Calibration is not a cosmetic step here — it's the difference between a model whose "70% confident" predictions win about 70% of the time, and one that's systematically over‑ or under‑confident. Both raw and calibrated probabilities were kept and scored separately to confirm the calibration step actually helped:

Raw vs. calibrated probabilities — forward test, 2025–2026 (N = 3,378)
SetAccuracyLog LossBrierAUCECE
Calibrated0.65570.61420.21360.71740.0134
Raw (uncalibrated)0.65360.61460.21380.71740.0165

Calibration improves Expected Calibration Error by ~19% at essentially no cost to discrimination (AUC unchanged) — confirmation the recalibration step is doing real work, not just relabeling the same predictions.

05 / Model vs. market

Well‑calibrated is not the same as competitive. The model loses to the market on every proper scoring rule but one.

Forward test, 2025–2026 · N = 3,378
MetricMarket odds aloneModel (calibrated)
Accuracy68.50%65.57%
Log Loss ↓ better0.58700.6142
Brier ↓ better0.20190.2136
AUC0.75180.7174
ECE ↓ better0.01600.0134

The market wins on discrimination and both proper scoring rules; the model is very slightly better calibrated in isolation. That one metric alone doesn't rule out a narrow, valuable disagreement in specific match‑ups — which is exactly what the four tests in the next section were built to check, rather than stopping here and calling it a loss.

Feature importance — base model, no market feature (xgb.importance)
FeatureGainCoverFrequency
Log_Rank_Diff_P10.46580.33150.2238
Active_Elo_Diff_P10.44910.35310.2844
Maturity_Diff_P10.03090.14450.1917
Momentum_Edge_P10.03060.10400.1429
Best_of_Numeric0.01170.03500.0633
Series_Weight0.00720.02040.0529
Round_Depth0.00470.01140.0411

Rank differential and surface Elo alone account for 91.5% of the model's total gain — momentum, maturity, and tournament‑context features are all doing real but marginal work on top of two dominant, market‑visible signals.

06 / Four independent falsification tests

Rather than trust one backtest, the same question was asked four different ways — and it kept returning the same answer.

1 · Horse‑race regression

Both the market's implied logit and the model's calibrated logit are regressed on the match outcome simultaneously. A model with genuine incremental value would show a positive, statistically significant coefficient on its own logit, even after the market's price is already accounted for.

TermEstimatep‑value
Market logit1.178< 0.001 ***
Model logit−0.1320.141 (n.s.)

The market's price is highly predictive on its own; the model's residual signal is statistically indistinguishable from zero, and its point estimate is negative.

2 · Segmented horse‑race

The aggregate result could still hide a real edge inside one specific slice of matches. The same regression was repeated within surface, tournament tier, round, and odds‑regime subsets.

SegmentNModel coef.p‑value
Surface: Hard2,088−0.0750.503
Surface: Clay1,003−0.2230.202
Surface: Grass287−0.3290.324
Series: Grand Slam599−0.1320.482
Series: Masters972−0.0900.578
Series: ATP5007320.0720.728
Series: ATP2501,060−0.3440.053
Round: Early2,476−0.1230.227
Round: Late (QF+)543−0.3220.200
Has strong favorite (<1.5)1,856−0.0610.620
Close match (1.5–2.5 both sides)1,360−0.2810.046

No segment shows a positive, significant model coefficient. One segment — genuinely close matches, both sides priced 1.5–2.5 — shows a significant negative coefficient: following the model's disagreement with the market in close matches is actively worse than following the market.

3 · Market‑aware model variant

A second model was trained with the market's own implied probability added directly as an input feature — to test whether the model could learn to correct residual market error rather than compete blind against it. That single feature absorbed ~80% of the model's total predictive gain, and a horse‑race test on its residual signal was still not significant (p ≈ 0.56). Giving the model the market's price made it mostly imitate that price rather than add anything beyond it.

4 · Systematic odds‑interval sweep

Rather than one hand‑tuned staking rule, every odds range from 1.00 to 3.00 was swept systematically with a single fixed rule: back the model's higher‑probability side whenever it shows positive expected value. Each bucket's return is shown with a bootstrapped 95% confidence interval — with only a few hundred bets per bucket, a single positive point estimate can easily be noise.

ROI by odds bucket, with 95% bootstrap CI — forward test, 2025–2026

CI entirely below zero CI crosses zero (inconclusive)
Only the widest, thinnest‑data bucket (2.51–3.00, n = 51) has a point estimate above zero — and its interval spans −29% to +42%, wide enough to be consistent with almost anything. Every bucket with real sample size either sits below zero or straddles it. The same sweep on the 2023–2024 calibration period (not shown) shows the identical pattern — no bucket clears zero on both periods, which is the bar this test was designed to require.
07 / Supplementary analysis

How strong a signal is ranking alone — with no model at all?

As a sanity check on how much of "beating the market" is realistically achievable from ranking data by itself, I measured the higher‑ranked player's raw historical win rate directly — no model, just the rank gap — across ~65,900 matches.

By rank‑gap tier

By match format

By tournament round

  • Favorite win rate rises steadily with rank gap: from 53.5% at a 1–5 rank gap to 78.1% at a 250+ gap.
  • Best‑of‑5 matches show a consistently higher favorite win rate than best‑of‑3 at every rank gap — e.g. 57.6% vs. 52.7% at a 1–5 gap, widening to 82.2% vs. 77.4% at 250+ — consistent with a longer format leaving less room for upsets.
  • Simply predicting "the higher‑ranked player wins" gets 65.4% overall accuracy (43,112 / 65,913), rising to 70.2% (42,767 / 60,926) when restricted to matches with valid, non‑extreme odds.

That 70.2% baseline sits close to the full XGBoost model's 65.6% test accuracy — a useful, humbling reference point for how much of the model's apparent skill is really just ranking information the market already prices in.

08 / Conclusion

Every test converges on the same answer — which is itself the useful result.

NO EXPLOITABLE EDGEvs. closing odds · confirmed

Across a market benchmark, a horse‑race regression, a segmented horse‑race, a market‑aware model variant, and a systematic odds‑interval sweep, every test converges on the same result: this feature set does not contain information beyond what is already priced into the bookmaker's closing odds.

This is a legitimate and useful finding, not a failed project. It rules out a specific, well‑motivated class of strategies through disciplined testing — rather than either assuming an edge exists after one good‑looking backtest, or giving up before checking properly.

It also points to what would be needed for a real edge: earlier (opening) lines before the market has fully priced in available information; structurally less efficient markets (lower tiers, thinner liquidity); or genuinely new information not reflected in rank or Elo — real‑time injury, fatigue, or news signals the closing line already absorbs but this dataset doesn't capture.

09 / Where this goes next

Ideas for what would actually move the needle — not just more feature engineering on the same inputs.

01

Test against opening lines, not closing lines

Closing odds have, by definition, already absorbed nearly all available public information. Any real edge is far more likely to survive against an opening price, before the broader market has finished reacting.

02

Move to a structurally less efficient market

Lower ATP tiers and Challenger‑level events see far less pricing attention and liquidity than Slams and Masters events — exactly where a modest, genuine informational edge would be easiest to find and hardest to arbitrage away.

03

Bring in information the market prices but this dataset doesn't

Recent injury or withdrawal news, fatigue from recent match load, and surface‑transition effects are all almost certainly baked into closing prices already — but aren't present as explicit features here. Sourcing them directly, rather than engineering more variants of rank and Elo, is the more promising direction.

04

Point‑by‑point / in‑play modeling

All of the above still targets a single pre‑match probability. A sequence model over point‑by‑point or set‑by‑set state would open up in‑play pricing, a market that moves fast enough that a small latency or attention edge is more plausible than in a closing pre‑match line.

05

Staking simulation as a stress test, not a strategy

Even with a confirmed null result, running a fractional‑Kelly staking simulation over the same forward‑test period would be a useful due‑diligence exercise — showing directly how quickly a "looks fine on average" edge can still produce a losing, high‑variance bankroll path when it isn't real.

Full pipeline (cleaning → features → model → calibration → validation) implemented in R — dplyr, lubridate, stringr, tidyr, xgboost, ggplot2, pROC. Source data: ATP men's tour match and odds history, 2000–2026.

tennis_pipeline_portfolio.R
